coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of oligo(ethylene glycol)-capped fluorescent carbon dots from glutamic acid for plant cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Zhaoyan
Qu, Yanning
Gao, Xiaotong
Mu, Changjun
Bai, Jiangping
Pu, Qiaosheng
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots are potential candidates for replacing traditional dyes for cell imaging, but their bulky sizes may hinder their passage through cell membranes, which may result in extra work to inject them into the cells. Here, a simple one-step method is proposed to synthesize brightly fluorescent carbon dots that can be endocytosed by intact plant cells and can thus directly dye the nucleus. The morphology, composition and effects of acidity and excitation wavelength on the photoluminescence of the obtained glutamic acid-based carbon dots were systematically investigated. The particles have a size of 4–8nm and possess large amounts of oligo(ethylene glycol) groups with a quantum yield of 30.7%. The material exhibited high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Confocal microscopy confirmed that the carbon dots prepared with the proposed method could dye HeLa cells within 1min and intact onion epidermal cells in 10min through endocytosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Oligo(ethylene glycol)
High quantum yield
Plant cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM images and (b) FTIR spectrum of carbon dots.
Confocal microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with carbon dots (a) and AO (b); and onion epidermal cells incubated with carbon dots for 10 and 60min (c and d) and AO (e) (λ=488nm).
Possible mechanism of the carbon dots formation.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of oligo(ethylene glycol)-capped fluorescent carbon dots from glutamic acid for plant cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gansu Key Laboratory of Crop Improvement and Germplasm Enhancement, College of Agronomy, Gansu Agricultural University, Lanzhou 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Gansu Key Laboratory of Crop Improvement and Germplasm Enhancement, College of Agronomy, Gansu Agricultural University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots are potential candidates for replacing traditional dyes for cell imaging, but their bulky sizes may hinder their passage through cell membranes, which may result in extra work to inject them into the cells. Here, a simple one-step method is proposed to synthesize brightly fluorescent carbon dots that can be endocytosed by intact plant cells and can thus directly dye the nucleus. The morphology, composition and effects of acidity and excitation wavelength on the photoluminescence of the obtained glutamic acid-based carbon dots were systematically investigated. The particles have a size of 4–8nm and possess large amounts of oligo(ethylene glycol) groups with a quantum yield of 30.7%. The material exhibited high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Confocal microscopy confirmed that the carbon dots prepared with the proposed method could dye HeLa cells within 1min and intact onion epidermal cells in 10min through endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots, which are a group of nanoparticles with carbonized cores, emerged as a new class of nanomaterials with high aqueous solubility, low environmental toxicity, good biological compatibility, strong and tunable photoluminescence, low photobleaching, lack of blinking, robust chemical inertness, and easy functionalization [1–3] and have become a competitive alternative for biological labeling, bioimaging and drug delivery [4,5]. However, due to their large sizes compared to traditional dyes, the introduction of carbon dots into cells may pose a challenge even though their cellular uptake, fluorescence brightness in a cellular environment and feasibility for in vivo imaging in mouse models have been investigated [4,6]. These carbon dots could penetrate cell membranes, but most of them accumulated in cell membranes and the cytoplasm, and only a small portion of these carbon dots could enter the cell nucleus [7–9], thus indicating difficult transport through membranes. Even for those that entered the nucleus, a long incubation time was required [9]. In addition, animal cells were selected as the targets for most of these investigations, and plant cells have rarely been employed.

In this work, to obtain an efficient, non-toxic and fluorescent nanocarbon that can be directly used for plant cell imaging, brightly fluorescent oligo(ethylene glycol)-capped carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of L-glutamic acid in the presence of nitric acid and glycol. The resulting carbon dots have a high quantum yield and tunable photoluminescence. More importantly, these carbon dots exhibit virtually no toxicity to living cells and can penetrate into and label the nucleus of animal cells in 1min. In addition, it is possible to dye intact plant cells within 10min.

Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots: Fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized in one step by hydrothermal treatment of L-glutamic acid with nitric acid as a carbonization agent and glycol as a passivation agent ( Scheme 1). Briefly, 1.5g of L-glutamic acid and 20ml of a solution containing 5mol/L HNO3 and 45% glycol were added to a PTFE-lined autoclave. The mixture was heated at 180°C for 10h and cooled to room temperature resulting in the formation of a dark brown solution. Centrifugation (11,000rpm, 1h) was used to remove the undispersed solids. Then, the supernatant was diluted appropriately for fluorescence measurements and cell imaging.

Characterization of fluorescent carbon dots: High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using a JEM-1200EX transmission electron microscope at 200kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Nicolet NEXUS 670 spectrometer at room temperature. Element analyses were performed on an Elementar Vario EL element analyzer. Microscopic fluorescence images were acquired with a Zeiss LSM 510 META confocal microscope with an excitation wavelength of 488nm. The fluorescence quantum yield was measured on an Edinburgh FLS 920 fluorescence spectrometer with sulfate quinine as a reference.

Cellular imaging: Onion epidermal cells and HeLa cells were chosen to demonstrate cellular imaging with the prepared carbon dots. Onion epidermis was peeled off of a fresh onion and immersed into 5ml of a carbon dot solution with a given concentration in a Petri dish and incubated for a predefined period of time at room temperature. Next, the carbon dot solution was removed, the cells were washed thrice with 1ml of phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 10mmol/L), and confocal fluorescent microscopic images were taken with excitation at 488nm. HeLa cells were seeded on glass cover slips, treated with 5ml of the carbon dot solution in a Petri dish for 1min and then washed thrice with 1ml of PBS prior to recording the confocal images. Both types of cells were stained with acridine orange (AO) as positive controls following the normal procedure.

The morphological and structural features of the prepared carbon dots were systematically characterized. As shown in the HRTEM images ( Fig. 1a), the carbon dots were spherical, and the diameters were in the range of 4–8nm. The carbon dots were well dispersed, and no aggregation was observed. FTIR spectra were used to identify the functional groups present in the particles (Fig. 1b). The peaks located at 3433 and 1083.8cm−1 were attributed to O–H and C–O stretching vibrations, which confirmed the presence of glycol units in the product. In addition, the formation of oligo(ethylene glycol) should be beneficial to biocompatibility and water solubility and play a critical role in plant cell wall endocytosis [10]. The elemental analysis of the prepared carbon dots (Found: C, 63.8; H, 4.5; N, 11.4; O, 20.3) differed from that of L-glutamic acid (C, 40.8; H, 6.2; N, 9.5: O, 43.5). In addition, an increase in the carbon content was additional evidence of carbonization. A significant decrease in oxygen indicated dehydration and decarboxylation. Due to the presence of ethylene glycol in the reaction mixture, it is expected that oligo(ethylene glycol) was formed, which is supported by the O–H, C–O and CH x vibrations in the IR spectra and the results from the elemental analysis.

Although a simple one-step procedure was adopted, the obtained nanoparticles exhibited the typical photoluminescent behavior of carbon dots prepared with other techniques [11,12]. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission peak shifted to longer wavelengths, and a different fluorescence color (from blue to red) was observed. The fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots was dependent on the pH (from pH 2 to 11), and the variation in the fluorescence intensity caused by pH was reversible. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity returned to the original value when the pH value was adjusted back to the original value. At an excitation of 360nm, the quantum yield measured was 30.7% when sulfate quinine was used as the reference. This value is among the highest reported for carbon dots. In-situ surface passivation of the formed carbon dots with oligo(ethylene glycol) and the unique structure of glutamic acid may be responsible for this excellent quantum yield, which is consistent with that reported by Hsu and Chang [13]. In addition, to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the prepared carbon dots, in vitro cell viability tests were performed by evaluating the growth of Escherichia coli K12 cells in the presence of the carbon dots at different concentrations. The cell growth was not affected when the concentration of carbon dots were less than 1500mg/L. This result indicated that the obtained carbon dots were non-cytotoxic to the live cells at a wide concentration range, which is in agreement with previously reported results [2].

The in vitro cellular imaging was performed with HeLa cells and onion epidermal cells ( Fig. 2). Fig. 2a shows the images of the HeLa cells after 1min of incubation with the carbon dots (300 times diluted). The carbon dots were taken up by the HeLa cells in a short period of time, and they successfully stained only the cell nucleus specifically. Onion epidermal cells were used to demonstrate the ability of the carbon dots to stain plant cells (Fig. 2c and d). Due to the presence of cell walls, staining plant cells was not as easy as staining animal cells [14]. However, after 10min of incubation with the carbon dots, the cell was clearly observed, and the brightness increased as the incubation time increased, which clearly indicated that the carbon dots penetrated the cell walls. As with the HeLa cells, the nucleus was primarily stained, but the cell wall was also stained after approximately 60min of incubation, which indicated that a portion of the carbon dots remained in the cell walls.

Highly fluorescent oligo(ethylene glycol)-capped carbon dots were prepared from L-glutamic acid, and the prepared material exhibited the typical fluorescence behavior of carbon dots. With the unique structure, which was due to the proper selection of initial reactants, the prepared carbon dots exhibited low cytotoxicity, and they were used for fluorescent imaging of both animal and plant cells. It is expected that specific labeling of a certain analyte with carbon dots should be possible after proper modification of the surface carboxyl groups with functional units.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175062 and J1103307), the Program for New Century Excellent Talents in University (NCET-08-0255), and Z. Wang acknowledges the support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2013-70).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.matlet.2014.05.016.
